coredata.dc:title: Sensitive fluorescence immunoassay on the basis of the fluorescence quenching effects of quantum dots for the determination of norfloxacin in animal-origin foods
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Gaoshuang
Su, Dan
Yu, Qingxiu
Zhao, Tao
Gao, Shan
Hao, Jianxiong
coredata.dc:description: A highly sensitive and reliable fluorescence immunoassay based on the fluorescence quenching (FQ) effect of quantum dots (QDs) was developed for the sensitive detection of norfloxacin (NOR) in animal-origin foods. Briefly, the enzymatic formation of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) in conventional ELISA effectively quenched the fluorescence of QDs through inner-filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and then the absorption signal of conventional ELISA was transformed to fluorescent signals, resulting in a higher sensitivity for NOR detection in animal-origin foods. The detection limit of NOR in this developed method was 0.20 μg L−1 in standard solution, which was 12-fold more sensitive than conventional ELISA (2.50 μg L−1). The results of specificity analysis revealed that the proposed method could not only be used for the detection of NOR, but also for the other 9 structural analogues. The recovery rates of NOR-spiked animal-origin foods ranged from 88.11 % to 108.16 %, with CV values of 1.95 %–18.03 %. In addition, the established method had good consistency with the commercial ELISA kits and HPLC, indicating the accuracy of the developed method for the detection of NOR in real samples. In conclusion, the established fluorescence immunoassay was sensitive and facile, and could be alternatively used for the determination of NOR residues in animal-origin foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NOR
QDs
Fluorescence quenching
Immunoassay
Animal-origin foods

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching effect of QDs based on HRP
Establishment of the QDs-based fluorescence ELISA
Assessment of fluorescence quenching effect between QDs and HRP substrates
Construction of QDs-based fluorescent quenching immunoassay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Project of Hebei Education Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Science and Technology Project of Hebei Education Department (QN2020177).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Evaluation of the quenching mechanism. (a) TEM of QDs. (b) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of QDs and absorption spectra of TMBox. (c) Fluorescence lifetime of QDs before and after the reaction with TMBox. (d) Fluorescence recovering of QDs from TMBox after the ultrafiltration (3500 Da, 4000 rpm for 5, 10, 15 min). (e) Plots of F0–F values versus HRP concentrations from 0 to 2.5 mU mL−1 (inset: its corresponding linear relationship from 0 to 0.002 mU mL−1).
Schematic for the developed fluorescence immunoassay based on fluorescence quenching effect of quantum dots for the detection of NOR in animal-origin foods.

Optimization of working conditions for the developed fluorescent immunoassay. (a) Optimization of the dilution ratio of NOR-HRP and QDs solution. (b) Optimization of the reaction times for the developed fluorescent immunoassay.
Quantitative detection of NOR using the traditional ELISA and the developed fluorescent immunoassay.
An Overview on Recently Reported Immunoassays and biomimetic immunoassay for the Detection of NOR.

row.entry: Biomimetic immunoassay based on graphene quantum dots and MIP
Chemiluminescence immunoassay based on a novel Nor-Biotin bifunctional ligand and SA-ALP technology
Immunosensor based on PAMAM dendrimer-encapsulated Au
Dual-readout immunoassay based on QDs-FM@ALP-SA and click chemistry
Fluorescent quenching immunoassay based on QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cross-reactivity of Anti-NOR antibody with other fluoroquinolones antibiotics.
Verification of the developed method for NOR detection in real samples using commercial ELISA kit and HPLC (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Not detected. The concentration was lower than the detection limits of this method, ELISA kit and HPLC.

head.ce:title: Sensitive fluorescence immunoassay on the basis of the fluorescence quenching effects of quantum dots for the determination of norfloxacin in animal-origin foods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Biology, Hebei University of Science and Technology, Shijiazhuang, Hebei 050018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Biology
Hebei University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Biology, Hebei University of Science and Technology, Shijiazhuang, Hebei 050018, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, P.R. China;

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly sensitive and reliable fluorescence immunoassay based on the fluorescence quenching (FQ) effect of quantum dots (QDs) was developed for the sensitive detection of norfloxacin (NOR) in animal-origin foods. Briefly, the enzymatic formation of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) in conventional ELISA effectively quenched the fluorescence of QDs through inner-filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and then the absorption signal of conventional ELISA was transformed to fluorescent signals, resulting in a higher sensitivity for NOR detection in animal-origin foods. The detection limit of NOR in this developed method was 0.20 μg L−1 in standard solution, which was 12-fold more sensitive than conventional ELISA (2.50 μg L−1). The results of specificity analysis revealed that the proposed method could not only be used for the detection of NOR, but also for the other 9 structural analogues. The recovery rates of NOR-spiked animal-origin foods ranged from 88.11 % to 108.16 %, with CV values of 1.95 %–18.03 %. In addition, the established method had good consistency with the commercial ELISA kits and HPLC, indicating the accuracy of the developed method for the detection of NOR in real samples. In conclusion, the established fluorescence immunoassay was sensitive and facile, and could be alternatively used for the determination of NOR residues in animal-origin foods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Norfloxacin (NOR) is an important group of synthetic broad spectrum antibiotics and has been widely used in human and veterinary treatment. However, the misuse of this chemical caused potential threaten to food safety and human health (Chen et al., 2021; Hu, Liu, et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2018). Sensitive and accurate instrument analysis methods, including capillary electrophoresis (CE) (He et al., 2019), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Kamal & El-Malla, 2019), mass spectrometry (MS) (Mohan et al., 2021), and liquid chrometography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Bao et al., 2021), were established to detect NOR in biological matrices. However, these methods require sophisticated instruments, complex pre-treatment processes, and professional operators, which are all not suitable for rapid on-site screening of NOR (Niu et al., 2017).

With the advantages of short detection time, strong specificity, low cost, large sample processing capacity and low requirements for instruments and equipment, immunoassay has attracted more and more attention of researchers’ (Zhao et al., 2020). Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is the most commonly employed immunoassay and shows advantages of high throughput (Wang et al., 2017). However, traditional ELISA always uses enzymes, typically horseradish peroxidase (HRP) or alkaline phosphatase (ALP) to catalyze the enzyme substrate into colored products, and showed moderate sensitivity due to lower signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio, which hinders its wide application in food analysis (Wu et al., 2019). To substitute absorption signals by fluorescence counterpart is a feasible, efficient and frequently employed strategy to obtain higher sensitivity and S/N ratio (Barroso et al., 2016; Yuan et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2019). Quantum dots (QDs), with unique advantages, such as large Stokes displacement, good optical stability, high quantum yield and adjustable emission wavelength (Bonilla et al., 2016; María et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2018), have been employed as signal labels for antibodies in immunoassay (He et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2022; Zhan et al., 2016). However, most of these methods showed similar sensitivity compared with ELISA, since these method eliminate the signal amplification of enzymatic reaction. Thus, we can speculate that a higher sensitivity could be obtained if both fluorescent signal readout and enzymatic amplification were used at the same time in immunoassay.

Therefore, a fluorescence immunoassay on the basis of the fluorescence quenching (FQ) effect of quantum dots (QDs) was proposed for sensitive determination of NOR in animal-origin foods in our study. In this strategy, QDs was used as signal probes, and the enzymatic formation of HRP in traditional ELISA effectively quenched the fluorescence of QDs through IFE and FRET, then the absorption signals of conventional ELISA were transformed to fluorescence signals, resulting in a higher sensitivity for NOR detection. As a conclusion, this study can provide a new idea for rapid, efficient and sensitive determination of NOR residues in animal-origin foods.

Fluoroquinolones standards, tetracycline, penicillin, chloramphenicol, streptomycin, gentamicin, sulfadiazine, horseradish peroxidase (HRP), n,n-dicyclohexyl carbodiimide (DCC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and ovalbumin (OVA) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Skim milk powder was supplied by Bo'ao Tuoda Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). HRP substrate solution A (peroxide solution) and substrate solution B (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine, TMB) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). And the substrate of HRP (pH = 4.5) contains TMB (2.5 mM) and H2O2 (10 mM). The commercial ELISA Kits were supplied by Reagent Technology Co. Ltd (Shenzhen, China). Anti-NOR monoclonal antibodies (McAbs) were supplied by Shandong Lvdu Bioscience and Technology Co., Ltd (Shandong, China). The quantum dots (QDs) were from Wuhan Jiayuan Quantum Dots Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China).

Fluorescence intensity spectra was recorded using a Spectra Max i3x microplate reader (Molecular Devices). Shimadzu UV-2300 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was employed to obtain ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra. Fluorescence emission and excitation spectral data of QDs was acquired using a F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Japan). The morphology of the nanoparticles was characterized by JEM-2010 FEF transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL Ltd., Japan) operated at 200 kV.

NOR-HRP was prepared using an activated ester method (Acaroz et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2018). Generally, 7 mg norfloxacin was dissolved into 300 μL n,n-dimethylformamide (DMF), then 22.70 mg n,n-dicyclohexyl carbodiimide (DCC) and 12.65 mg N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were accurately weighed and dissolved in the above solution. The solution was stirred continuously at room temperature and dark for 4–6 h. After the reaction, the reaction solution was centrifuged (5 °C, 10,000×g, 5 min) to eliminate the precipitation. Under ice bath condition, the upper activated ester solution was slowly added drop by drop to 3 mL sodium bicarbonate solution dissolved with 10 mg horseradish peroxidase (HRP) (130 mmol L−1, pH 8.1), the reaction solution was stirred overnight at 4 °C, and then transferred to a dialysis bag. Dialysis was carried out at 4 °C with phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (0.01 mol L−1, pH 7.4). The obtained NOR-HRP (3.78 g L−1) was characterized using an UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer. The maximum absorbance for NOR-HRP at 405 nm gave a significant peak shift compared with the maximum absorbance for NOR at 350 nm, verifying the presence of NOR-HRP conjugation (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching effect of QDs based on HRP
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quenching effect of QDs based on HRP was conducted as follows: 1 mL 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate was mixed with HRP (0–2.5 mU mL−1). Meanwhile, 20 μL diluted QDs solution was added at the same time. Then, the reaction solution was incubated for 15 min at 37 °C. The fluorescence was measured after incubation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the QDs-based fluorescence ELISA
ce:section.ce:para: 1 μg well−1 antibody (3.5 μg mL−1) in a carbonate buffer (CB) was added into a 96-well microtiter plate. After incubation overnight at 4 °C, the plate was rinsed with PBST (0.2 % tween 20 in PBS, pH = 7.4) solution for 3 times. 200 μL of 0.5 % skim milk was added as blocking solution and incubated at 37 °C for 1h. After 3 successive rinsing steps, a series of NOR standard solutions (50 μL well−1), and the same volume of NOR-HRP were added to each well, respectively. After washing plate with PBST for 5 times, 100 μL the mixed tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate and 20 μL appropriate amount of QDs was added. After incubation for 15 min, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 340/525 nm excitation/emission wavelength.

Cross-reaction was conducted to assess the antibody specificity towards NOR. In this experiment, 17 norfloxacin structural analogues, including gatifloxacin (GAT), enrofloxacin (ENR), ciprofloxacin (CIP), Enoxacin (ENX), ofloxacin (OFL), lomefloxacin (LOM), fleroxacin (FLE), sarafloxacin (SAR), nalidixic acid (NAL), sparfloxacin (SPA), pipemidic acid (PIP), oxolinic acid (OXO), cinoxacin (CIN), pefloxacin (PEF) and mbofloxacin (MBO), flumequine (FLU) and pefloxacin methanesulfonate (PM), and 6 other types of antibiotics including tetracycline (TET), penicillin (PEN), chloramphenicol (CHL), streptomycin (STR), gentamicin (GEN) and sulfadiazine (SUL) were selected to calculate the cross-reaction value as follows:
(1) CR % = IC50 (NOR)/IC50 (compound) × 100
Where CR indicates cross-reaction, IC50 indicates half-maximum inhibition concentration.

In this experiment, animal-origin foods (confirmed negative samples by HPLC) purchased from local supermarkets were selected as testing samples, including milk, tissue samples (chicken, beef, pork) and aquatic products (shrimp and sea bass). The samples were treated according to our previous study (Hu et al., 2015). For tissue samples and aquatic products, 1 g homogenized specimen was transferred in a 50-mL centrifuge tube, and 2 mL extract mixture (acetonitrile and 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH solution, 8:2, V/V) was added. The target detection substance in the sample was fully extracted by shaking for 5 min, and then diluted with PBS (pH 7.4) for detection. For milk sample, it was diluted with PBS (pH 7.4) at a appropriate ratio, and then detected directly.

Milk samples without NOR were spiked with NOR at 1, 5 and 50 μg/L, tissue samples and aquatic products were spiked with NOR at 2, 10 and 100 μg/kg, then the spiked samples were simultaneously analyzed by the proposed assays, commercial ELISA kit and HPLC. HPLC analysis conditions are given in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of fluorescence quenching effect between QDs and HRP substrates
ce:section.ce:para: In our study, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching between QDs and HRP substrates (TMBox) was assessed. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the TEM image of QDs showed that their average sizes were approximately 4 nm with low degree of agglomeration. And the maximum absorption of TMBox at 370 nm overlaps the excitation wavelength of QDs at 340 nm (as shown in Fig. 1b), thus we suspect that an effective fluorescence inner-filter effect (IFE) would occur between TMBox and QDs. Besides, it is worth noted that the absorption peak of TMBox at 640 nm also overlaps the maximum emission wavelength of QDs at 525 nm, indicating that there maybe weak fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between TMBox and QDs. To further confirm the existence of FRET, fluorescence quenching effect was distinguished by measuring whether fluorescence lifetime attenuation occurred before and after QDs reaction with TMBox. As shown in Fig. 1c, the fluorescence lifetime decreased by 23.67 %, indicating that there is a dynamic quenching between QDs and TMBox. In addition, we separated QDs from the quenching system by ultrafiltration test and determined the fluorescence intensity of QDs to verify whether the fluorescence of QDs can be restored (Dong et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 1d, the fluorescence intensity of QDs recovered with increasing time from 0 min to 15 min, indicating that no non-fluorescent complex was formed, further confirming dynamic quenching mechanism. Therefore, we can conclude that the fluorescence quenching effect produced in the experiment is not only caused by a single IFE, but also caused by FRET between QDs and TMBox. (Kamal & El-Malla, 2019; Yu et al., 2015; Zu et al., 2017).

In order to ensure the stability of the developed method, interfering factors were evaluated. The background fluorescence and interfering fluorescence were investigated, since it was a prerequisite for the detection system. As shown in Fig. S2a, TMBox had no emission under UV excitation. While, TMB showed a weak fluorescence emission peak at 450 nm, due to the resonance interaction at different positions of the molecules as well as the formation of the quinoid structure with a planar configuration in the excited state. Further study was then conducted to investigate the influence of TMB on the fluorescence of QDs, and the data in Fig. S2b demonstrated the fluorescence of QDs had no change after reacted with TMB, indicating that TMB had no affect the fluorescent signals of QDs. Thus, the results demonstrated that TMBox and TMB have almost no influence on the fluorescent outputs of QDs. As reported previously, the fluorescent signals of QDs are sensitive to oxidants (Christensen et al., 2011), pH (Zhang et al., 2020), etc. And it is worth noted that the HRP substrate (pH 4.5) contains TMB (2.5 mM) and H2O2 (10 mM). To avoid the signal interference caused by the detection system matrix (not by the HRP-catalyzed products), the fluorescent stability of QDs in the matrix was systematically assessed. Fluorescence intensities of QDs dispersions at various pH values are displayed in Fig. S2c. The findings demonstrated that the fluorescence stability of QDs was excellent in a pH range of 3–9, indicating that QDs could act as a probe for HRP-induced fluorescence quenching, since the optimal pH range of HRP activities is 3–5 (Jiang et al., 2016). The effect of H2O2 on the fluorescent intensity of QDs was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. S2d, the fluorescence of QDs could be quenched when the concentration of H2O2 exceeded 100 mM, which was relatively higher than the actual H2O2 concentration in HRP substrate. Moreover, the fluorescence of QDs was very stable during the IFE process, especially within 25 min (as shown in Fig. S2e), demonstrating the excellent photo-stability of QDs. Meanwhile, the possibility that QDs had enzyme-mimicking properties was also evaluated. The absorbance values of HRP substrates with QDs (diluted by 5000 times) were recorded, and there was no significant OD change for HRP substrates within 10 min, as shown in Fig. S2f, indicating QDs exhibited no catalytic activities toward HRP substrates.

Then the FQ effect was characterized by determining the fluorescent variants of QDs compared with varying concentrations of HRP (0–2.5 mU mL−1). More catalytic product (TMBox) will be generated with the gradual addition of HRP, and more fluorescence quenching will occur between QDs and TMBox, resulting in the increase of F0–F. However, the reaction system reaches saturation after HRP concentration reached to 0.002 mU mL−1, since all the substrates have been catalyzed. As shown in Fig. 1e–a linear relationship was observed between QDs (F0–F) fluorescence intensity and HRP concentration (0–0.002 mU mL−1). And the fitting linear equations in Fig. 1e inset are: Y = 604.69ln(x) + 6212.3, (R2 = 0.986).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of QDs-based fluorescent quenching immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: A novel fluorescent immunoassay based on fluorescent quenching of QDs and conventional ELISA was established to determine NOR in animal-origin foods in our study. In the strategy, the HRP-formed products (TMBox) in conventional ELISA would efficiently quench the fluorescence of QDs via the IFE and FRET (as shown in Fig. 2 ). If there was no NOR residue in samples, high amount of TMBox will be produced, causing significant fluorescence queching of QDs, and if there was high amount of NOR residue in samples, less TMBox will be produced, causing less fluorescence change. Hence, the NOR concentration is inversely proportional to the reduced IFE-based fluorescent signals of QDs.

The following parameters of the developed method were optimized, including dilution ratio of NOR-HRP, dilution ratio of QDs and enzymatic reaction time. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the optimum dilution ratio of NOR-HRP was 1:4000, dilution ratio of QDs was 1:5000. The optimum enzymatic reaction time was 15 min, since the lowest IC50 can be obtained for that long time (As shown in Fig. 3b).

The standard curve of HRP-based fluorescence ELISA was developed under the optimized conditions. According to the previous study (Liu, Qin, et al., 2021; Sheng et al., 2016; Yuan et al., 2011), the assay sensitivity was evaluated using the IC50, which is defined as the concentration of target giving a 50 % inhibition. Also, the LOD was calculated as IC15. As shown in Fig. 4 , the inhibition rate was plotted against the log concentration of NOR, where inhibition rate was determined as follows:
(2) Inhibition Rate ( %) = 1-(Fblank-F)/(Fblank-F0) × 100 %
Where Fblank, F0 and F indicate the fluorescent signals of the blank well (no mAbs), fluorescence intensity of wells without NOR and NOR-spiked wells, respectively. The fluorescence intensity change linearly depends on the concentration of NOR in the range from 0.28 to 21.18 μg L−1with a regression coefficient of 0.9956. And the IC50 of the developed fluorescence ELISA was 2.43 μg L−1, and the IC15 of the developed fluorescent ELISA was 0.20 μg L−1, which was about 12-fold lower than the IC15 of the conventional ELISA (2.50 μg L−1). And the IC50 of the conventional ELISA was 21.98 μg L−1. The analytical feature of our established method was compared to several immunoassays and biomimetic immunoassay using molecular imprinting technique for NOR determination (Bunkoed et al., 2020; Cheng et al., 2022; Han et al., 2021, 2022; Hu, Gao, et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2022; Liu, Xu, et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2018; Zong et al., 2019a). As shown in Table 1 , the developed method showed ideal LOD (calculated as IC15) and detection time. More importantly, in contrast to other forms of fluorescent immunoassay, this assay does not need covalent bonds between fluorescent probes (QDs) and analytes (or enzymes) (Fang et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2017). The complex procedure of QDs labeling and surface modification is prevented through the fluorescence quenching effect. In addition, this assay does not need complex chemical synthesis process compared with the mentioned biomimetic immunoassay, showing advantages of organic reagent-free and environment-friendly. As a conclusion, the method could be an alternative method for the determination of NOR.

To evaluate the specificity of the method, 17 norfloxacin structural analogues and 6 types of other antibiotics were selected for the fluorescence ELISA analysis. As shown in Table 2 , we found that GAT (17.21 %), ENR (11.86 %), CIP (6.78 %), ENX (3.50 %), LOM (3.00 %), FLE (2.01 %), SAR (1.63 %), NAL (1.53 %) and SPA (1.18 %) showed a relative high cross-reactivity with NOR. This might be due to the fact that they share the basic chemical structure of bicyclic ring (Kocsis et al., 2016). Therefore, it is concluded that the fluorescence immunoassay can not only be used for the detection of NOR, but also for the detection of other 9 norfloxacin structural analogues.

Immunoassay is rapid and convenient for sample analysis, however, many chemical compounds presenting in the samples, such as protein, fattiness, and others, might affect the recognition of antibody and targets and other performance of the immunoassay, which will reduce the sensitivity and reliability of the assay. One efficient way to overcome the matrix effect of the samples was diluting the extract with standard solution, such as PBS, and the comparing the standard curve with a calibration curve generated in the sample matrix. If the two curves are superimposed, the matrix effect is not significant. And the samples can be analyzed according to the standard curve.

In this study, we found that calibration curve generated in milk sample were superimposed with standard curve, when the sample were diluted fivefold with PBS, and under this condition the matrix interferences could be minimized (Fig. S3a). While, for tissue samples extract with tenfold dilution using PBS could minimize the matrix interferences (Figs. S3b–f).

Recovery studies were then performed by assessing specimens spiked with 3 concentrations of NOR at high, middle and low levels. For milk, the spiked concentration was 1, 5 and 50 μg L−1. For tissue samples and aquatic products, the spiked concentration was 2, 10 and 100 μg L−1, respectively. The average recoveries ranged from 81.11 % to 119.76 % with a coeffificient of variation (CV %) ranging from 1.05 % to 18.03 % (Table 3 ), indicating the developed method had an acceptable precision level for NOR quantifification.

The accuracy of the developed method was then validated by commercial ELISA kit and HPLC. And the results demonstrated that the proposed method exhibited a good agreement both with commercial ELISA kit and HPLC, suggesting a good practicability of the developed fluorescence immunoassay. In conclusion, the established novel immunoassay could be used for quantitative and sensitive determination of NOR in real food specimens.

In conclusion, a fluorescence immunoassay technique was developed with simple signal production for the high-sensitive determination of NOR residues in animal-origin foods. In this strategy, QDs-based fluorescence quenching was combined with conventional ELISA system based on the catalytic amplification of HRP/TMB. The findings demonstrated that the detection limit of NOR in this developed method was 0.20 μg L−1 in standard solution, which was 12-fold more sensitive than conventional ELISA (2.50 μg L−1). The data of specificity analysis revealed that the proposed method could not only be used for the detection of NOR, but also for the detection of other 9 structural analogues. The recovery rates of NOR spiked animal-origin foods ranged from 88.11 % to 108.16 %, with CV values of 1.95 %–18.03 %. In addition, the established method had high consistency with the conventional ELISA kits and HPLC, suggesting a good accuracy of the developed method. In conclusion, the proposed fluorescence immunoassay provides a flexible, scalable and versatile platform, and could be alternatively used for the quantitative determination of NOR residues in animal-origin foods. Meanwhile, the developed method provides alternative strategy for sensitive detection of other trace hazardous substances, such as pesticide or mycotoxin, in food matrix. It is worth noted that, the natural enzymes (HRP) used in this study was easy to be influenced by heat, acid and alkali. In addition, the content of natural enzymes in organisms is very low, which is difficult to obtain in large quantities, resulting in high production cost, short shelf life, and reduced activity during conjugation reaction. Therefore, we could do some work on the study of novel fluorescence quenching immunoassay based natural enzyme substitution, such as peroxidase-like nano-enzymes etc., in the future study.
This article does not contain any studies with laboratory animals or human participants performed by any of the authors.

Gaoshuang Hu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Dan Su: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Qingxiu Yu: Methodology. Tao Zhao: Formal analysis. Shan Gao: Supervision. Jianxiong Hao: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Science and Technology Project of Hebei Education Department (QN2020177).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fbio.2024.103681.
